coredata.dc:title: Gamma-irradiation assisted green synthesis of water hyacinth-derived carbon dots for enhanced photoselective film applications

Seesuea, Chuleekron
Sangtawesin, Tanagorn
coredata.dc:description: High quantum yield carbon dot (CD) was synthesized from water hyacinth leaves (WH) through ionizing gamma radiation, eliminating the need for heat or chemical treatments. The study investigated the effects of different gamma doses (50, 100, 150 kGy) on the resulting CD's optical properties, particle sizes, and surface chemistry. The 50 kGy dose produced CD (CD50) with a remarkable quantum yield of 48.3 %. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-CD50 composite films demonstrated enhanced root growth in Chinese convolvulus by 19 % and were non-toxic to human cells. These composites also proved effective for food packaging applications, as evidenced by the reduced color changes in strawberries under UV light exposure. This study introduces a green synthesis approach for transforming invasive water hyacinth into a high-value CD, with promising applications in electronics, catalysis, and sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gamma irradiation
Carbon dot
Water hyacinth
Photoselective composite film
Cytotoxicity

We would like to thank RMUTT Central Lab, Institute of Research and Development, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, for facility support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The appearance of CD50, CD100, and CD150 were prepared at 50, 100 and 150 kGy, respectively.
Zeta potential of CD50, CD100, and CD150.
TEM images and (a) CD50, (b) CD100, and (c) CD150.
(a) FTIR and XPS spectra of CD50, CD100, and CD150: (b) elemental concentration (% wt.), (c) survey, and high-resolution spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s, and (f) O1s.
(a) UV–Vis absorption, (b) quantum yield (Φ), (c, e, g) fluorescence emission, and (d, f, h) normalized fluorescence intensity spectra of CD50, CD100, and CD150 (1.0 mg/ml) in deionized water.

The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of CD50, CD100, and CD150 under (a) visible light at room temperature, (b) UV irradiation at 365 nm, (c) in buffer medium at different pH, and (d) in the presence of NaCl (0–2 M).
MTT-based cytotoxicity assay of (a) HepG2 cells and (b) L929 cells after 24 h incubation with different concentrations of CD50, CD100, CD150 (0.1, 1, 10, 100, and 1000 μg/mL).
DPPH's radical scavenging activity of different concentrations ofascorbic acid, CD50, CD100, and CD150.
(a) The contact angle, (b) FTIR (c) transmission spectra, and (d) light transmission composition of PVA and CD-PVA composite films.
The effects of incorporating CD into PVA film for the cultivation of Chinese Convolvulus include (a) stem circumference, (b) stem height, and (c) root length.
The strawberries' color difference (ΔE value) using 50CD-PVA and PVA films compared with the control condition (without films), testing under 365 nm for two days.
The chemical compositions from the deconvolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s.
Comparison of the previous CDs from the biomass.

head.ce:title: Gamma-irradiation assisted green synthesis of water hyacinth-derived carbon dots for enhanced photoselective film applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Photochemical and Electrochemical Materials Research Unit

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Thailand Institute of Nuclear Technology (Public Organization), Nakorn Nayok 26120, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Thailand Institute of Nuclear Technology (Public Organization)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Thailand Institute of Nuclear Technology (Public Organization), Nakorn Nayok 26120, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High quantum yield carbon dot (CD) was synthesized from water hyacinth leaves (WH) through ionizing gamma radiation, eliminating the need for heat or chemical treatments. The study investigated the effects of different gamma doses (50, 100, 150 kGy) on the resulting CD's optical properties, particle sizes, and surface chemistry. The 50 kGy dose produced CD (CD50) with a remarkable quantum yield of 48.3 %. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-CD50 composite films demonstrated enhanced root growth in Chinese convolvulus by 19 % and were non-toxic to human cells. These composites also proved effective for food packaging applications, as evidenced by the reduced color changes in strawberries under UV light exposure. This study introduces a green synthesis approach for transforming invasive water hyacinth into a high-value CD, with promising applications in electronics, catalysis, and sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes), recognized as a highly invasive species, proliferates rapidly with yields of 100–120 tons per hectare per year, posing significant environmental challenges [1–5]. It clogs waterways, increases evaporation, and serves as a channel for greenhouse gas emissions from aquatic environments [1–5]. The primary strategy for managing water hyacinth involves removing and disposing of affected areas, which experience considerable management costs. Due to its high carbon (40 %) and low nitrogen (1.2 %) content, along with minimal lignin (<10 %), cellulose (about 14 %), and hemicellulose (around 65 %), water hyacinth is an ideal candidate for producing carbon-based materials such as biochar, activated carbon, biofuel, and carbon dots [2–4].

The carbon dot (CD) is carbon nanoparticles with strong fluorescence emission, UV light absorption, nontoxicity, nonblinking, and photobleaching resistance [6–8]. Hydrothermal-solvothermal treatment, microwave irradiation, pyrolysis, and chemical oxidation processes are commonly used for CD synthesis [9–11]. Tan et al. synthesized the blue emitted from sago industrial waste by carbonization at 250–450 °C (ambient air) and applied it for heavy metal detection [12]. Aji et al. produced a CD from mangosteen peel with urea via pyrolysis at 200–300 °C with blue emission [13]. Suryawanshi et al. synthesized CD from dried Neem leaves through pyrolysis at 1000 °C under argon and acid oxidation (H2SO4, HNO3), yielding CD (2 % quantum yield, Φ) for Ag+ sensing [14]. Wang et al. also employed pyrolysis at 700 °C under N2 gas and strong acid oxidation (H2SO4 and HNO3) to create silica nanoparticles grafted with CD from rich silica rice husks for cell imaging applications [15]. Zhao et al. created lignin-derived CD (Φ = 16.6 %) for Cu2+ detection using a hydrothermal at 160 °C. Furthermore, Zhao et al. produced lignin-derived CD as the fluorescence probe for Cu2+ using a hydrothermal method at 100–180 °C for 6 h [16].

Gamma irradiation emerges as an eco-friendly technique for carbon dot (CD) synthesis, where gamma radiolysis splits water molecules into reactive components that fragment lignocellulose by severing ester and glycosidic linkages in lignin and cellulose [17], respectively. This process can lead to the degradation and cross-linking of natural polymer chains under specific conditions like accumulated dose and oxygen presence [17]. Wechakorn et al. successfully produced 10 % sugar-derived CDs using gamma radiolysis at 25–45 kGy doses in an ammonia solution in a low-oxygen environment. Similarly, they synthesized CD from water hyacinth stalks with a 2.5 % of Φ at 80 kGy in deionized water under normal atmospheric conditions [18]. Although promising in initial experiments, further refinement of gamma irradiation parameters is crucial for enhancing the optical qualities of CD.

CDs' high UV absorption and photoconversion properties can enhance agricultural and food packaging applications by influencing plant growth and photosynthesis, with UV light (UVC, UVB, UVA) impacting different species [19,20]. For example, UV reduction boosts growth, yield, and photosynthetic pigments in monocots like sorghum and wheat, while reducing UVB increases tomato lycopene and affects flavonoid and phenolic acid levels in Brassicales [19]. Utilizing CD in polymer films for greenhouses and tunnels allows controlled UV exposure, converting UV to visible light for UV-sensitive plants. CD-polymer films, particularly those using PVA certified by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) for food and meat packaging, enhance food shelf-life due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, and cost-effectiveness [21,22]. Zhang et al. created a PVA-CD film from coffee waste for food pH and Al3+ detection with antioxidant properties [23]. Patil et al. developed a UV-blocking PVA film with CD from tea waste, showing that UV protection varies with CD content [24]. Zhao et al. found that PVA with 0.5 % CD extends jujube shelf-life to 6 days, offering antioxidant, and antimicrobial benefits and minimal color change under UV storage [25].

In the present study, we employed gamma radiolysis as an innovative approach to producing a CD from water hyacinth leaves, eliminating the need for hazardous chemicals, external heat sources, and residual waste generation. This method promotes the polymerization and cross-linking of natural polymers, and small molecules can occur under specific irradiation conditions, such as accumulated dose, dose rate, and media conditions. We conducted a detailed investigation into the effects of varying gamma radiation doses (50, 100, and 150 kGy) on the characteristics of the produced CD. Moreover, this research explores the viability of incorporating the synthesized CD into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film composites, assessing their potential applications in agricultural films and food packaging.

Water hyacinth leaves were collected from a river near the Thailand Institute of Nuclear Technology. All chemicals and reagents were used without purification. Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, MW ≈ 145,000 kDa, diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH), and quinine sulfate monohydrate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Functional groups in chemicals were studied using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 27). Element components and chemical structure were determined by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (AXIS Ultra DLD, Shimadzu). Particle sizes were investigated by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Scientific TALOS F200X). UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected on a C-7100 Peak UV/Visible spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were performed on JASCO FP-8500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The zeta potential and particle size were measured on a Malvern zeta sizer Ultra. Contact angle was performed by Contact angle meter, SL250 series, KINO scientific instrument.

Water hyacinth (WH) was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 48 h. The WH powder was sieved with 200 mesh sieves. Four grams of WH powder was mixed with 200 ml of DI water in glass bottles and sonicated for 1 h. The glass bottles were sealed with parafilm and irradiated with gamma radiation (Gamma Chamber 5000, BRIT India, the dose rate of 1.9 kGy/hour) at the dose of 0, 50 (CD50), 100 (CD100), and 150 kGy (CD150). The exposure times required for doses of 50 kGy, 100 kGy, and 150 kGy were 26.3 h, 52.6 h, and 78.9 h, respectively. After that, the mixtures were filtered to separate solid and liquid parts. The supernatant was centrifuged (4000 rpm/min) 2 times for 30 min, then filtered by a 0.2 μm-nylon filter. The solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h and freeze-dried to provide CD as the yellow solid. The CD derived from the WH powder pre-irradiated at 50, 100, and 150 kGy were named CD50, CD100, and CD150, respectively.

HepG2 (liver cancer) and L929 (mouse fibroblast) cells (5,000cells/well) were seeded into a 96-well plate at 200 µL and incubated at 37 °C in 5 % CO2. After 24 h, the cells were treated with various concentrations of the CD (0.1, 1, 10, 100, and 1000 µg/mL) for 24 h. Next, 10 µL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 4 h. After removing the medium, the formazan crystals were solubilized in 150 µL of DMSO. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a 96-well plate reader, and the obtained value was assumed to represent the number of viable cells. After treatment withsamples and untreated cells, the cell morphology was visualized under a phase-contrast microscope. The cytotoxic activity was determined in the triplicate experiment for determining % cell viability. The percentage of cell viability was calculated for each compound using Equation (1) [26]:
(1)
% C e l l
v i a b i l i t y =
A t
/
A 0
× 100
Where At is the absorbance of experimental cells at 570 nm, and A0 is the absorbance of control cells at 570 nm.

The antioxidant capacities of CD50, CD100, and CD150 were evaluated through their DPPH radical scavenging abilities at concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.05 mg/ml, with ascorbic acid employed as the comparative standard. Solutions of each CD sample were prepared at a concentration of 0.2 mg/ml in deionized water. A DPPH solution (1.0 mM) in ethanol was added to these solutions, and the volume was adjusted to 2 ml. The mixtures were incubated at ambient temperature for 30 min in a dark environment to prevent light-induced degradation. Following incubation, the absorbance at 517 nm was determined using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The percentage of DPPH radical scavenging in the CD samples was calculated relative to the standard reference using a specified equation (Eq. (2)).
(2)
DPPH
radical
scavenging
activity =
A 0
-
A S
A 0
× 100
where As is the absorbance of the sample and A0 is the absorbance of the blank (without the CD solution).

The mixture between PVA (7.5 g) and DI water (100 ml) was heated at 90 °C for 2 h. The 100 mg of as-synthesized CD: CD50, CD100, and CD150 was dissolved in 20 ml of DI water and sonicated for 30 min. Then slowly drop the CD solution into the PVA mixture and stir at 90 °C for 1 h. The slurry was casted on the 27 × 18 cm mold and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h. The PVA film incorporated CD50, CD100, and CD150 were named PVA, CD50-PVA, CD100-PVA, and CD150-PVA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon Dot-PVA composite film for agricultural film test
ce:section.ce:para: Chinese convolvulus (Home Garden) was soaked in water for 10 h and then transferred to wet paper tissues for six days. The seeds were then continuously grown in a seedling tray (20 × 24 cm, 12 holes) with a 4.5 cm height of sandy soil in each hole. The seedling tray was inside a 22.0 × 31.3 × 25.5 cm box with three synthesized CD-PVA composite film sides. To control the grown environment, the film boxes (PVA, CD50-PVA, CD100-PVA, and CD150-PVA) were put in a 72 × 84 × 67 cm opaque box with an LED natural light bulb (Lamptan, 11 W, and 1,100 lm). The light bulb was turned on for 12 h and off for 12 h. The temperature in the box was recorded during the day. Chinese convolvulus was watered every five days with 15 ml of DI water in each hole. The number and height of survival Chinese convolvulus were counted on the 7th day. The stem sizes, weight, root height, and leave number were recorded on the 25th day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD/PVA composite film for food packaging test
ce:section.ce:para: This experiment aimed to determine the effectiveness of CD-PVA composite film in retaining strawberry quality and preventing spoiling. The experiment was applied by placing individual strawberry samples in a paper cup and covering them with PVA, CD50-PVA, CD100-PVA, and CD150-PVA, as shown in Fig. S1. The samples were then exposed to UV light (365 nm) and silica gels to absorb moisture during an experiment. Strawberry samples were pictured daily to monitor color and physical changes. Specific points on the strawberry images were marked (Fig. S2), and color values (L a b) were measured using Adobe Photoshop. ΔE was calculated to identify color differences, as shown in Fig. S3. The large E indicates the high level of color shifts.

In contrast to traditional pyrolysis techniques, gamma radiation engages with water hyacinth (WH) biomass at the atomic level, leading to atomic ionization and excitation. In aqueous conditions, the gamma radiation is predominantly absorbed by water, generating active radicals such as hydroxyl (HO•) radicals, hydrated electrons, and hydrogen atoms. The hydroxyl (HO•) radicals play a pivotal role in scavenging protons from molecules possessing weak C-H or O-H bonds, thereby facilitating the breakdown of lignocellulose chains. As the reaction advances, these radicals engage in a chain transfer mechanism with other molecular species, propelling a chain reaction forward. In the case of hemicellulose and cellulose, initial depolymerization yields oligosaccharides, which subsequently undergo glycosidic bond cleavage and rearrangement to form 1,4-anhydro-D-xylopyranose. The specific irradiation conditions also influence factors such as the degree of dehydration, decarboxylation, aromatization, and intramolecular condensation in effective CD formation. The CD was successfully synthesized from the water hyacinth (WH) supernatant by one-step gamma radiolytic. The as-prepared CD at 50 kGy (CD50), 100 kGy (CD100), and 150 kGy (CD150) appear as a yellow–brown solid (Fig. 1 ), and their product yields are 4.6 %, 4.6 %, and 5.2 %, respectively, which were calculated from Eq. (3) [27].
(3)
% y i e l d =
weightofdriedCD
weightofusedWHpowder
× 100
These findings indicate that the variation in gamma irradiation doses had a marginal impact on the yields of CD production. To further refine the synthesis process, the optimization of gamma irradiation doses was investigated to enhance specific properties such as quantum yield, photostability, and fluorescence emission within the visible spectrum.

The zeta potential of as-synthesized CD was elucidated in deionized water, as shown in Fig. 2 . The surface charge of CD50 (−19.97 mV), CD100 (−14.95 mV), and CD150 (−19.50 mV) indicates that they are unstable and tend to aggregate to form large, aggregated particles.

In addition, TEM was used to investigate the morphology of CD50, CD100, and CD150, as shown in Fig. 3 . They exhibit spherical particles with a broad size contribution (Fig. S4); particle sizes greater than 60 nm (darker contrast) and smaller than 10 nm (brighter contrast) were found, as shown in Fig. 3. The larger particles exhibited darker contrasts, indicating that these particles are thicker layers according to a high degree of aggregation. It is in good agreement with the zeta potential results. These findings reveal that CD100 tends to have more aggregation to large particles than CD50 and CD150, which is in good agreement with the lowest negative zeta potential and may be explained later by the surface chemical functional group. Moreover, the higher dose of gamma irradiation produced more active radical species and more excellent glycosidic bond cleavage. Therefore, the particle sizes of CD150 less than 10 nm were found that better than those of CD100 and CD50. The radiolytic degradation was complex, combining aromatization and intramolecular condensation, increasing crystallinity and reducing crystallization [28]. Therefore, the size distribution of CD150 was almost found in the wide range of 2–140 nm, which is higher than that of CD100 and CD50, respectively.

FTIR technique was used to explore the surface functional groups of as-synthesized CD, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The FTIR spectra of CD50, CD100, and CD150 contain several peaks: O–H/N–H stretching (3200–3300 cm−1), O–H bending (1394 cm−1), C–N stretching (1236 cm−1), and C–O stretching (1045 cm−1). It suggests that as-synthesized CD had hydroxyl or amine groups. The C–H stretching (2959 cm−1) and C=C stretching (1585 cm−1) were also found, indicating sp2 -hybridized carbon conjugation.

The carbon contents of CD150 (58.9 %) were more significant than CD100 (57.3 %) and CD50 (54.4 %), as shown in Fig. 4b. Similarly, the lower oxygen contents were found in CD150 (29.7 %). The elemental analysis of CD was determined from XPS analysis. C1s (285 eV), N1s (399 eV), and O1s (532 eV) were the majority elements of CD, as shown in Fig. 4c. The highest nitrogen contents were found in CD100 (4 %). From the high-resolution spectra of C1s, the carbon components of all as-synthesized CD consist of C=C/C–C (285 eV), C–O/C–N (287 eV), C=O/C=N (288 eV), and O=C–O (289 eV). For O1s spectra, all sample shows similar peaks: C–O–C (533 eV), O–C (532 eV), COOH (531 eV), and C=O (534 eV). In the case of N1s spectra, two peaks of graphitic-N and pyrrolic-N were presented at 400 eV and 401 eV, respectively. Notably, 20.3 % of C=O moieties were found in CD150, but 8.2 % and 7.9 % for CD100 and CD50, respectively (Table 1 ). As the gamma irradiation dose increases to 100 kGy for CD100, there is a significant decrease in pyrrolic nitrogen (77.6 %) and a corresponding increase in graphitic nitrogen (22.4 %). This trend continues for CD150, which is exposed to the highest gamma dose of 150 kGy, showing a slight reduction in pyrrolic nitrogen (77.1 %) and a stable amount of graphitic nitrogen (22.9 %) compared to CD100.

With increasing gamma irradiation doses from 50 kGy to 100 and 150 kGy, the transformation of pyrrolic nitrogen to graphitic nitrogen in CD samples likely occurs through the irradiation-induced modification of defects within the lattice. Higher doses introduce more energy, breaking existing C–N bonds in pyrrolic structures and creating new defect sites [17,29,30]. This facilitates the reconfiguration of nitrogen atoms from less stable pyrrolic forms, bonded in five-membered rings, to more stable graphitic forms, where nitrogen substitutes for carbon in the hexagonal lattice [31,32]. The process is driven by the system's tendency towards lower energy and higher stability configurations, with defect migration and healing mechanisms playing a role in rearranging atoms and incorporating nitrogen into graphitic positions [31,32]. This suggests that increased gamma irradiation can effectively alter the nitrogen doping configuration in graphene by enhancing the stability and integration of nitrogen atoms within the lattice.

UV-Vi’s absorption spectra of CD50, CD100, and CD150 solution (1.0 mg/ml) were examined in deionized water (Fig. 5 a). The maximum absorption bands of CD50, CD100, and CD150 were at 226, 229, and 228 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of sp 2 -hybridized carbons, respectively. The second absorption peak attributed to the η → π * transition of the CD50, CD100, and CD150 carbonyl group were at 280 to 295 and 320 nm, respectively. Notably, the higher gamma dose may cause a blue shift of the η → π* transition, which results from oxidation surface formation during high-dose irradiation.

The CD solution shows blue fluorescence emission (λem) under UV light (365 nm). The fluorescence emission spectra of CD50, CD100, and CD150 (1.0 mg/ml) were elucidated at the excitation (λex) of 240–500 nm, as shown in Fig. 5c, 5e, and 5g, respectively. The λem of CD50, CD100, and CD150 are similar, approximately 450 nm (λex = 360 nm). The normalized fluorescence intensity spectra of CD50, CD100, and CD150 are shown in Fig. 5d, 5f, and 5h, respectively. It was found that CD50, CD100, and CD150 have excitation-dependent fluorescence emission in a visible light region. Quantum yield (Φ) of as synthesized-CD was performed in 1 M H2SO4 compared to the quinine sulfate monohydrate (QS, Φ = 54.0 %) as the standard reference at λex = 360 nm (Fig. 5b). The Φ of the CD solution was determined via the slope from the linear plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance between CD and QS, Quantum yields of CD50 (48.3 ± 1.4 %) is higher than CD100 (18.9 ± 0.1 %), and CD150 (23.5 ± 0.6 %) respectively. The Φ of CD50 shows much greater than other reports, including water hyacinth and other biomass, as shown in Table 2 .

The photostability and photobleaching of CD50, CD100, and CD150 were also investigated. The CD solution (1.0 mg/ml) was excited at 360 nm, and the emission intensity was measured at 445 nm for the photostability under visible and UV light (365 nm) at room temperature (Fig. 6 ). The relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of CD50 (99 %), CD100 (98 %), and CD150 (96 %) solutions under visible light for two weeks barely changed. However, after exposure to UV light (365 nm) for 1 h, the CD solution degraded slightly, with the F/F0 of CD50, CD100, and CD150 being 90 %, 92 %, and 86 %, respectively. These results suggested that CD50, CD100, and CD150 show acceptable photostability and no photobleaching under UV–visible light. In addition, the photostability of CD solution was elucidated in NaCl solution and pH buffer solution. Fig. 6c presents the photostability of CD50, CD100, and CD150 at the pH 3–11 buffer solution. The fluorescence intensity of CD is high emission at pH 5 and pH 7. However, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly at strong acids (pH 3) and strong basic conditions (pH 9 and pH 11) due to the deprotonation of the surface functional groups [42]. The NaCl solution (0–2 M) does not affect the F/F0 intensity of CD50, CD100, and CD150, as shown in Fig. 6d. These results indicate that CD prepared from gamma irradiation shows high photostability and no photobleaching, which could be further applied in bioimaging in living cells.

Since CD are widely regarded as biocompatible nanomaterials and fluorescence bioimaging in live cells, the cytotoxicity of CD50, CD100, and CD150 in HepG2 and L929 cells was evaluated using the MTT assay. The microscope images of the cells treated with and without different concentrations of CD50, CD100, and CD150 (0.1, 1, 10, 100, and 1000 μg/ml) for 24 h in both HepG2 and L929 cells show no significant change in shape, as shown in Figs. S5 and S6, respectively. The relative cell viabilities of CD50, CD100, and CD150 (100 μg/ml) for HepG2 cells were calculated to be 92.3, 98.5, and 101.2 %, respectively (Fig. 7 a). The cell viabilities of CD50, CD100, and CD150 (100 μg/ml) for L929 cells were also calculated to be 95.8 %, 98.0 %, and 97.6 %, respectively (Fig. 7b). However, the CD50, CD100, and CD150 solution cell viability at 1000 μg/ml in HepG2 cells exhibited 83.0, 85.7, and 86.4 %, respectively (Fig. 7a). The cell viabilities of CD50, CD100, and CD150 solution at 1000 μg/ml in L929 cells exhibited 84.1, 86.7, and 91.5 %, respectively (Fig. 7b). Because of the nitrogen-containing functional groups on the as-synthesized CD, a high concentration of CD50, CD100, and CD150 enhanced cell toxicity [43]. These results indicated that CD50, CD100, and CD150 derived from water hyacinth up to 100 μg/ml in HepG2 and L929 cells had low cytotoxicity (the survival rates > 92 %). Since the as-synthesized CD was proven to have low toxicity and good biocompatibility, it can be applied for bio-related applications such as cell imaging, bio-sensing, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics.

The DPPH radical scavenging abilities of CD50, CD100, and CD150 were evaluated as presented in Table 3 . The IC50 values of CD50, CD100, and CD150 were determined to be 17.2 ± 0.4, 29.1 ± 0.4, and 21.5 ± 0.3 μg/ml, respectively, the performance of CD50 (83 %), CD100 (83 %), and CD150 (70 %) in radical scavenging was compared to that of ascorbic acid, as depicted in Fig. 8 . The antioxidant activity of the CD samples is closely linked to their hydrogen or electron donation capacity to neutralize DPPH radicals, greatly affected by their surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl (–OH) groups. The proposed mechanism for radical scavenging involves the transfer of hydrogen from the CDs' surface functional groups, including carboxyl (COOH), hydroxyl (OH), and amino (NH2) groups, to the DPPH radicals. High-resolution O1s spectra support this, showing a higher relative area of COOH groups in CD50 and CD100 compared to CD150, which corresponds with their enhanced DPPH scavenging activity.

The surface morphology of CD-PVA composite films was investigated using a digital camera and SEM, as shown in Fig. S7. There is no significant difference in surface morphologies between PVA, CD50-PVA, and CD100-PVA. However, the CD150-PVA surface was much smoother than the others. Moreover, the effect of incorporating CD on the contact angle of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films was investigated, as shown in Fig. 9 a. The contact angle of water on the PVA film was 77°, indicating that the water droplet on the film's surface exhibits relatively high resistance to spreadand tends to form a spherical shape. It can be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the PVA film. The contact angles of CD50-PVA, CD100-PVA and CD150-PVA were 74°, 54°, and 44°, respectively. This significant reduction in the contact angle leads to the enhancement of the wetting property of the CD-PVA film since the higher hydrophilic of the surface film results from the hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl (OH) or carboxyl (COOH) groups on the as-synthesized CD.

Fig. 9b shows the FTIR spectra of CD50-PVA, CD100-PVA, and CD150-PVA. The OH/N-H stretching vibrations, as indicated by the peaks around 3286 cm−1 for PVA, show a slight shift in frequency in the composites, indicating interactions between PVA and CD samples. The shifts are not consistently proportional to the irradiated levels, suggesting that factors other than treatment intensity might influence the bonding interactions within the composites. CD50-PVA, CD100-PVA, and CD150-PVA show peak shifts to 3282 cm−1, 3271 cm−1, and back to a lesser shift at 3282 cm−1, respectively, demonstrating that the interaction effect does not linearly correlate with the irradiation doses.

The investigation into CD-PVA films' UV–visible light transmission properties revealed promising results for their application as UV-blocking materials (Fig. 9c and d). While the pure PVA film, characterized by its amorphous structure and transparency, displayed the highest UV transmission among the films tested, the CD50-PVA film exhibited superior UV-blocking performance, effectively blocking 51.7 % of UV-A, 88.7 % of UV-B, and an impressive 98.0 % of UV-C radiation. The intermediate CD150-PVA and CD100-PVA films showed moderate reductions in UV transmission, indicating that varying the gamma dose of CD production influences the film's UV-blocking capabilities. The outstanding performance of the CD50-PVA film is likely attributed to the seamless integration of CD materials, which have shown remarkable UV absorption properties. These findings underscore the potential of modified PVA films as effective UV-blocking materials and suggest avenues for enhancing UV-blocking efficiency in various applications, including outdoor furnishings, automotive windows, and protective clothing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CD-PVA composite film for agricultural film
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization results demonstrate that the CD-PVA composite films exhibit remarkable UV-blocking properties and photoconversion capabilities. As mentioned in the experimental part, the significant benefits of incorporating CD into the PVA film were demonstrated for cultivating Chinese convolvulus. The survival rates of plants grown with CD50-PVA film (83.3 %) were notably higher than PVA film (66.7 %) and without film (41.7 %), suggesting that CD provides an additional protective barrier, shielding the Chinese convolvulus from adverse environmental conditions and effectively UV-blocking. The improved survival rate is precious in agricultural practices where maintaining high plant survival is crucial for successful crop production.

The stem circumference measurements also show that plants grown with CD50-PVA and PVA film had similar values of 0.65 cm. In comparison, plants without film exhibited a smaller circumference (0.60 cm), as shown in Fig. 10 a. The incorporation of CD did not hinder the flexibility or growth of the stems, suggesting that the CD is well-integrated within the PVA film matrix. Furthermore, the height analysis (Fig. 10b) demonstrated that the plants grown with CD50-PVA film achieved the most significant height (13.50 cm), followed by plants without film (13.10 cm) and those grown with PVA film (10.20 cm). The considerable increase in plant height with CD-modified PVA film indicates enhanced overall growth and development. This result could be attributed to improved nutrient absorption facilitated due to the significantly most extended root length (4.3 cm) when using CD50-PVA film as the UV-blocked film, as shown in Fig. 10c. The results suggest that as-synthesized CD in the film enhances root development and potential nutrient uptake. Overall, the results emphasize the positive effects of incorporating CD into PVA film for cultivating Chinese Convolvulus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CD-PVA composite film for fruit packaging
ce:section.ce:para: To emphasize the importance of utilizing CD-PVA film for fruit packaging, the quality preservation and the shelf-life extension for strawberries were also investigated under UV light (365 nm) by color difference (ΔE). The ΔE value of PVA film was 1282.9, and the reduction of ΔE was observed for CD50-PVA film (1163.8), as shown in Fig. 11 . It suggests that an incorporated CD50 into the PVA film possesses light-modulating properties. On the other hand, an absence of film resulted in rapid deterioration and undesirable changes, rendering the strawberries unsuitable for consumption (ΔE = 1398.9). The UV blocking and conversion to visible light of as-synthesized CD contribute to a slower degradation rate of the strawberry color. This finding highlights the potential of as-synthesized CD derived from water hyacinth via green radiation process incorporated PVA films in effectively reducing fruit spoilage and extending shelf life.

This study reveals a sustainable method to convert water hyacinth leaves into high-quality carbon dots (CD) using ionizing gamma radiation at 50, 100, and 150 kGy doses. As-synthesized CD at 50, 100, and 150 kGy can block UV light, have excellent fluorescence emission and have no toxicity to human cells. The optimal dose is at 50 kGy, giving the particle size of 66.3 nm and an impressive quantum yield of 48.3 %. The CD50-PVA film composites were used for UV-blocking film for Chinese convolvulus plants and strawberry packaging. With CD50-PVA film, the root of Chinese convolvulus plants is boosted by 19 %, and the color degradation of strawberries is less under UV light (365 nm).

Kanokorn Wechakorn: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Threeraphat Chutimasakul: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Thipthanya Duangmanee: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Bordin Weerasuk: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sakchai Laksee: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Piriya Kaeopookum: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Chuleekron Seesuea: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Tanagorn Sangtawesin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by IAEA (Coordinated Research Project, F22081), Thailand Institute of Nuclear Technology (Public organization), Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), and National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF). We would like to thank RMUTT Central Lab, Institute of Research and Development, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, for facility support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crcon.2024.100240.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
